title: Common Linear Patterns Are Rare
speaker: Nina Kamčev
abstract: Several classical results in Ramsey theory (including famous theorems of Schur, van der Waerden, Rado) deal with finding monochromatic linear patterns in two-colourings of the integers.  Our topic will be quantitative extensions of such results.  A linear system L

proof_stages.label: Ramsey multiplicity and the random benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from classical Ramsey patterns and reframes unavoidable monochromatic configurations as a multiplicity-minimization problem, with random two-colorings providing the benchmark density.

proof_stages.label: Finite-field definition of commonness
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes the finite-field model F_q^n, defines a homogeneous linear equation to be common when every two-coloring has at least the random density 2^{1-k} of monochromatic solutions, and explains the asymptotic and relaxation conventions.

proof_stages.label: Single-equation classification and graph analogy
proof_stages.summary: Known results are recalled: odd-variable equations are common by cancellation, while for even k common single equations are exactly those whose coefficients can be paired as additive inverses, paralleling graph commonality phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Extension from equations to systems
proof_stages.summary: Commonness is extended from one equation to a matrix Lx=0, with the same random benchmark because solutions still have k coordinates.

proof_stages.label: Four-variable obstruction to commonness
proof_stages.summary: The first main result says that systems containing a four-term-arithmetic-progression type two-equation/four-variable subsystem, more generally a 2-by-4 subsystem with arbitrary coefficients, are uncommon; the proof starts with a universal bad coloring for the small subsystem and then pseudorandomizes it to survive embedding in a larger matrix.

proof_stages.label: Even-girth critical-equation criterion
proof_stages.summary: The second main result introduces girth and critical equations of a linear system and proves that, for even girth, nonmatchability of all critical equations forces the whole system to be uncommon.

proof_stages.label: Certificate and superposition reduction
proof_stages.summary: A more general black-box criterion is stated: if one coloring makes the summed excess counts of all critical equations negative, then a modified coloring makes the whole system uncommon, reducing system commonness to Fourier-analyzable single-equation data.

proof_stages.label: Extensions, limitations, and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion and questions discuss odd-girth versions, two-equation systems, progress over F_2, analogues of the Sidorenko/property problem, possible transfer to graph commonality, minimization beyond uncommonness, and r-color variants.

examples.note: This concrete two-equation system illustrates how Gaussian elimination identifies girth four and a unique nonmatchable critical equation, making Theorem 3 applicable.

examples.note: The mixed example, with some matchable and some nonmatchable critical equations, illustrates that the stated nonmatchability criterion is not a characterization and that common systems still occur.
